content.text: For advanced readersThe above rules around creation, initialization, and destruction are guarantees. That is, objects must be created and initialized no later than the point of definition, and destroyed no earlier than the end of the set of the curly braces in which they are defined (or, for function parameters, at the end of the function).In actuality, the C++ specification gives compilers a lot of flexibility to determine when local variables are created and destroyed. Objects may be created earlier, or destroyed later for optimization purposes. Most often, local variables are created when the function is entered, and destroyed in the opposite order of creation when the function is exited. We’ll discuss this in more detail in a future lesson, when we talk about the call stack.

The above rules around creation, initialization, and destruction are guarantees. That is, objects must be created and initialized no later than the point of definition, and destroyed no earlier than the end of the set of the curly braces in which they are defined (or, for function parameters, at the end of the function).In actuality, the C++ specification gives compilers a lot of flexibility to determine when local variables are created and destroyed. Objects may be created earlier, or destroyed later for optimization purposes. Most often, local variables are created when the function is entered, and destroyed in the opposite order of creation when the function is exited. We’ll discuss this in more detail in a future lesson, when we talk about the call stack.

In actuality, the C++ specification gives compilers a lot of flexibility to determine when local variables are created and destroyed. Objects may be created earlier, or destroyed later for optimization purposes. Most often, local variables are created when the function is entered, and destroyed in the opposite order of creation when the function is exited. We’ll discuss this in more detail in a future lesson, when we talk about the call stack.

For advanced readersIf the object is a class type object, prior to destruction, a special function called a destructor is invoked. In many cases, the destructor does nothing, in which case no cost is incurred. We introduce destructors in lesson15.4 -- Introduction to destructors.

content.text: Key insightNames used for function parameters or variables declared in a function body are only visible within the function that declares them. This means local variables within a function can be named without regard for the names of variables in other functions. This helps keep functions independent.

Names used for function parameters or variables declared in a function body are only visible within the function that declares them. This means local variables within a function can be named without regard for the names of variables in other functions. This helps keep functions independent.
Best practiceDefine your local variables as close to their first use as reasonable.
Define your local variables as close to their first use as reasonable.

This style is suboptimal for several reasons:The intended use of these variables isn’t apparent at the point of definition. You have to scan through the entire function to determine where and how each variable is used.The intended initialization value may not be available at the top of the function (e.g. we can’t initializesumto its intended value because we don’t know the value ofxandyyet).There may be many lines between a variable’s initializer and its first use. If we don’t remember what value it was initialized with, we will have to scroll back to the top of the function, which is distracting.This restriction was lifted in the C99 language standard.

If we don’t remember what value it was initialized with, we will have to scroll back to the top of the function, which is distracting.

content.text: This restriction was lifted in the C99 language standard.
Best practiceWhen a variable is needed within a function:Use a function parameter when the caller will pass in the initialization value for the variable as an argument.Use a local variable otherwise.
When a variable is needed within a function:Use a function parameter when the caller will pass in the initialization value for the variable as an argument.Use a local variable otherwise.

list: Use a function parameter when the caller will pass in the initialization value for the variable as an argument.Use a local variable otherwise.
Use a local variable otherwise.

content.text: Key insightReturn by value returns a temporary object (that holds a copy of the return value) to the caller.
Return by value returns a temporary object (that holds a copy of the return value) to the caller.
